text:
Hygroscopic growth of aerosol particles in a German mountain forest during fall We measured the hygroscopic growth of aerosol particles at 90% relative humidity, their cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity and their size resolved chemical composition in the Thüringer Wald mountain forest in central Germany during fall 2010. The median values of the hygroscopic growth factor (GF) at dry conditions ( κ) derived from hygroscopicity measurements of particles with dry diameters of 50, 75, 100, 150, 200 and 250 nm were 0.14, 0.14, 0.17, 0.21, 0.24 and 0.28, respectively. We investigated the closure between hygroscopic growth factors derived from the horizontal tandem differential mobility analyser (HTDMA, κHTMDA) and those derived from the chemical composition of particles (κchem) using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) model. While the size averaged chemical composition led to an overprediction of κHTMDA by 30% to 40% for particles with dry diameters of 100 and 150 nm, good agreement between κchem and κHTMDA was achieved by using the size resolved chemical composition. Possible reasons for the observed discrepancies are evaporation of NH4NO3 in the HTDMA, which could lead to lower growth factors, and the assumption of the ZSR model concerning the organics, i.e. their volume fraction and the relationship between their volume fraction and κ. For the latter we derived a relationship between the hygroscopicity of the organics (κorg) and the oxygen to carbon ratio (O:C) of the organic mass, given by κorg = 0.19 × (O:C) − 0.03.

The κ derived from CCN measurements was found to be approximately 30% higher than those derived from hygroscopic growth (excluding the hydrophilic mode). Reasons for this discrepancy include effects due to high surface tension, solution non-ideality, semivolatile gases partitioned onto particles and effects due to partially soluble or insoluble components. Great care is then needed when extrapolating data derived under sub-saturated HTDMA conditions to the saturation level required for CCN activation. A closure study is performed between the κ derived from CCN analysis (κCCN) and the κ derived from laboratory measurements (κCCN, chem) where the latter was shown to be highly predictable based on a combination of size-segregated analysis of the components that comprise CCN activity and the ZSR mixing rule.
